text:
We describe the enantioselective hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides using a combination of an iridium(III) photocatalyst, a Brønsted acid and a chiral tetrapeptide thiol. The multicatalyst system gives highly enantioselective access to a variety of products bearing different protected β-amino-alcohol frameworks that are formed in good yields and with excellent enantioselectivities (up to 97 er). The key step that establishes the stereochemistry of the products is a critical hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) event in which the chiral thiol delivers one of the two hydrogen atoms to each C-centered radical in a highly discriminatory fashion.

Our initial goal was to improve the catalyst. In a detailed structure-selectivity relationship investigation, we probed the effects of changing peptide sequences and different olefin substrates to find that a tetrapeptide catalyst was the most effective. An extensive experimental and theoretical mechanistic investigation into the structure-selectivity relationship of the catalyst demonstrated that the high enantioselectivity and high levels of substrate recognition were the result of a cooperative noncovalent binding interactions set that included hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, and London dispersion forces. This paper is one of the first to explore asymmetric radical catalysis in detail, and demonstrates the role of noncovalent binding interactions in open shell processes and the stereochemistry that they yield.
